blocks.content: The apartment was quiet. Ethan was at the paint table — not painting, just sitting with the lamp on and Cassandra's canvas still drying at the edge. The streak of blue she'd made on purpose was there in the upper right corner, and he'd been looking at it for the past hour the way you look at something that means more than it should.
His phone lit up at 9:47.
I just found blue paint on my elbow. A completely different elbow than I thought was involved.
He smiled at the ceiling before he typed back.
It gets everywhere. First rule of painting.
You could have warned me.
You seemed like someone who needed to find out on their own.
That's either very perceptive or very convenient.
Both, probably.
There was a pause long enough that he wondered if she'd put the phone down. Then:
I left my painting there. Just realized.
I know. I'm looking at it.
Is it as bad as I think?
The cloud is still having a moment. But the awning turned out.
I feel like you're being diplomatic about the cloud.
I'm an artist. Diplomacy is part of the service.
I'm going to need an honest review at some point. A real one. Not the version where you find something generous to say about everything.

Okay. Honest: I like it. It has the thing beginners lose by their fifth painting, which is that they actually looked instead of just trying to be correct. There's something real in the way you did the street. You weren't trying to make it pretty. You were trying to make it true.
A longer pause.
That's the nicest thing anyone has said about something I made.
I mean it. The cloud just needs to decide what it's doing.
The cloud has range.
The cloud has chaos. That's different.
You sound like my high school art teacher. She used to say every bad element was just a misunderstood element.
Was she right?

Mostly. She was also the only teacher I had who stayed after class without making it feel like an obligation. Like she actually wanted to be there. She retired a few years ago and sent me a card that said "keep making messes on purpose." I still have it.
That's a good thing to hold onto.
Yeah. I think so too.
Another pause, different from the earlier ones. The kind that comes when a conversation has found its real register.
Hey.
Yeah.
Today was good. I know that's simple and maybe I should say something more complete about it. But today was just — really good.
He typed and deleted two different responses before he found the right one.
Yeah. It was.
I'll see you soon.
You will.

He set the phone down. He didn't pick up a brush. He sat with the lamp on and her painting at the edge of the table and the particular feeling of someone who has let something matter — and found, for once, that they didn't want to take it back.
